However, standard OPD typically generates full rollouts during training, which is computationally expensive and may expose the student to unreliable teacher feedback at late rollout positions, especially during early training. We identify the rollout horizon as a key bottleneck in OPD that substantially impacts training efficiency. Unlike Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), OPD does not require a complete trajectory or a final answer reward to provide learning signals. This observation suggests that full rollouts may not always be necessary for effective OPD. Motivated by this insight, we propose two simple horizon-control strategies: Progressive OPD (POPD), which gradually expands the rollout horizon during training, and Truncated OPD (TOPD), which permanently performs distillation on reliable truncated rollouts. Experiments on mathematical reasoning show that POPD improves the training efficiency of OPD by up to 3$\times$, while TOPD matches OPD performance using only 10\% of the rollout horizon, leading to substantial wall-clock and memory reductions. These results demonstrate that controlling the rollout horizon offers a simple and practical path to more efficient OPD.

</details>


### [217] [Consolidating Rewarded Perturbations for LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31494)
*Zheyu Zhang,Shuo Yang,Gjergji Kasneci*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CoRP consolidates rewarded perturbations into a single model, improving performance efficiently without multiple inference passes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiency of using multiple forward passes for ensembling in post-training language models, the paper proposes a method to consolidate rewarded perturbations into a single deployable model.

Method: CoRP (Consolidating Rewarded Perturbations) uses a gradient-free approach involving reward-weighted aggregation, compatibility-aware reweighting, and a validation gate, without backpropagation through the language model.

Result: CoRP improves the base model by 8.1 points on average across tasks, outperforming RandOpt with fewer perturbations and matching much of the ensemble's gain with a single forward pass.

Conclusion: CoRP provides an efficient and deployable alternative to ensembling, achieving significant performance gains while reducing computational overhead.

Abstract: Post-training of language models is commonly framed as a sample-score-update loop implemented by gradient descent. A recent line of work, exemplified by RandOpt, relocates this loop to weight space, sampling Gaussian perturbations around a pretrained model and ensembling the top-K rewarded specialists at inference. While competitive with PPO and GRPO under matched training compute, this prediction-level ensemble incurs K forward passes per test example and does not extend cleanly to free-form generation. We ask whether the rewarded population can instead be folded into a single deployable model, replacing the inference-time ensemble with one consolidated update. A split-half analysis over 25 model-task pairs reveals reproducible low-rank structure in every case. We turn this geometry into CoRP (Consolidating Rewarded Perturbations), a gradient-free operator that combines reward-weighted aggregation, compatibility-aware reweighting, and a held-out validation gate, with no gradient flowing through the language model. Across five language models from 0.5B to 8B and five tasks covering math, code, and creative writing, CoRP improves the base model by 8.1 points on average. Using one tenth of RandOpt's perturbation budget, CoRP exceeds single-inference RandOpt by 6.5 points and recovers more than half of the gain of the 50-pass majority-vote ensemble, at one forward pass per test example.

</details>


### [218] [Reliable Multilingual Orthopedic Decision Support from Clinical Narratives: Language-Aware Adaptation and Verification-Guided Deferral](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31512)
*Danish Ali,Li Xiaojian,Sundas Iqbal,Farrukh Zaidi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a reliability-oriented framework for classifying multilingual orthopedic notes in low-resource healthcare settings, comparing various models and achieving strong performance with IndicBERT-HPA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenges of multilingual orthopedic decision support in low-resource settings, where clinical narratives have specialized terminology, mixed scripts, and language-dependent patterns.

Method: Framework compares multilingual transformer encoders, DistilBERT, zero-shot LLMs, and domain-adaptive IndicBERT-HPA with language-aware adapter heads. Evaluation includes per-class performance, ROC-AUC, AUPRC, and robustness under different data distributions.

Result: IndicBERT-HPA outperforms other models, achieving an averaged Macro-F1 of 0.8792, Macro-AUROC of 0.894, and AUPRC of 0.902 under natural clinical prevalence. A selective-verification layer improves accuracy and coverage.

Conclusion: The study supports reliable multilingual clinical decision support with explicit deferral, demonstrating the effectiveness of task-adapted encoders over zero-shot LLMs.

Abstract: Multilingual orthopedic decision support remains challenging in low-resource healthcare settings, where clinical narratives contain specialized terminology, mixed scripts, incomplete evidence, label imbalance and language-dependent documentation patterns. This article presents a reliability-oriented framework for classifying free-text orthopedic notes in English, Hindi and Punjabi. We compare task-aligned multilingual transformer encoders, a task-fine-tuned DistilBERT baseline, zero-shot instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs) and a domain-adaptive encoder, IndicBERT-HPA. IndicBERT-HPA augments IndicBERT with language-aware orthopedic adapter heads to support clinically relevant multilingual representation learning. Evaluation extends beyond aggregate accuracy to per-class performance, ROC-AUC, AUPRC, expected calibration error, cross-language stability and robustness under controlled balanced and natural-prevalence distributions. The evaluated zero-shot LLMs remain substantially less effective than task-adapted encoders for closed-set classification, with language-dependent instability. Under natural clinical prevalence, IndicBERT-HPA achieves the strongest overall performance, reaching an averaged Macro-F1 of 0.8792, Macro-AUROC of 0.894 and AUPRC of 0.902. We further implement a deterministic selective-verification layer combining confidence gating, evidence-consistency checking and language-risk screening. On a randomly selected held-out 5,000-record subset, it achieves 84.4% selective accuracy and 0.76 selective Macro-F1 at 72.3% coverage, compared with 71.5% accuracy and 0.65 Macro-F1 for accept-all prediction. These results support reliability-oriented multilingual clinical decision support with explicit deferral.

</details>


### [219] [If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31514)
*Adrian de Wynter*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper challenges the assumption of anthropomorphic attributes in LLMs by demonstrating their non-uniqueness through a neural network trained on Age of Empires II, advocating for explicit measurement criteria and a 'null' assumption in experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critique the uncritical attribution of human-like qualities (e.g., morality, understanding) to LLMs and highlight the potential for misinterpretation based on the system's substrate.

Method: The authors build and train a simple neural network on Age of Empires II to show that anthropomorphic attributes can appear in any powerful substrate, like LEGO or the Greater Boston Area.

Result: Anthropomorphic attributes in LLMs are non-unique and dependent on the substrate, requiring explicit criteria for empirical discussion to avoid circular or uninformative conclusions.

Conclusion: The paper proposes a 'null' assumption of LLM non-uniqueness over anthropomorphism, emphasizing the need for clear measurement standards in evaluating LLM capabilities.

Abstract: Much research has been carried out on large language models (LLMs) and LLM-powered agentic workflows. However, many works within the field state emergence of, ascribe to, or assume, generalised anthropomorphic attributes to them (e.g., morality or understanding of natural language). Our goal is not to argue in favour or against the existence of these attributes, but to point out that these conclusions could be incorrect. For this we build and train a simple neural network on the videogame Age of Empires II, and note that any entity in a sufficiently-powerful substrate, such as LEGO or the Greater Boston Area, could also present such attributes. Hence, the purported anthropomorphic attributes of LLMs are empirically non-unique: although some properties (e.g., responses to prompts) could remain constant, others, such as the interpretation of their perceived behaviour, might change with the substrate. Thus, any empirically-grounded discussion requires explicit measurement criteria; otherwise the interpretation is left to the representation. We then show that assuming that these attributes exist or not in a system, independent of the substrate and in a generalised way, leads to either circular or uninformative conclusions, regardless of the experimenter's viewpoint on the subject. Finally we propose a 'null' assumption, where one assumes LLM non-uniqueness instead of assuming anthropomorphic attributes to set up an experiment, along with examples of it. We also discuss potential objections to our work, briefly survey the field, and prove that \textit{Age of Empires II} is functionally- and Turing-complete.

</details>


### [220] [UniAudio-Token: Empowering Semantic Speech Tokenizers with General Audio Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31521)
*Yuhan Song,Linhao Zhang,Aiwei Liu,Chuhan Wu,Sijun Zhang,Wei Jia,Yuan Liu,Houfeng Wang,Xiao Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: UniAudio-Token enhances semantic speech tokenizers with general audio perception while maintaining speech ability, using structured supervision and a content-aware gating mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the acoustic blindness of semantic speech tokenizers by enabling general audio perception without losing speech capabilities.

Method: Introduced Semantic-Acoustic Primitives (SAP) for structured audio decomposition and Semantic-Acoustic Equilibrium (SAE) for adaptive acoustic detail restoration.

Result: Achieves comprehensive universal audio representations and high-fidelity speech generation, outperforming baseline tokenizers in understanding and generation tasks.

Conclusion: UniAudio-Token serves as a unified audio interface, balancing linguistic and acoustic information, with publicly released code and models.

Abstract: Semantic speech tokenizers have become a widely used interface for Audio-LLMs, owing to their compact single-codebook design and strong linguistic alignment. However, their focus on linguistic abstraction induces acoustic blindness, limiting their applicability beyond speech-centric tasks. We propose UniAudio-Token, a framework that empowers semantic tokenizers with general audio perception without compromising speech ability. Instead of altering the semantic paradigm, UniAudio-Token mitigates its information loss through two key innovations: (1) Semantic-Acoustic Primitives (SAP) provide structured supervision by decomposing audio into linguistic content, vocal attributes, and auditory-scene primitives; and (2) Semantic-Acoustic Equilibrium (SAE) introduces a content-aware gating mechanism that adaptively restores fine-grained acoustic details from shallow layers. Extensive evaluations show that UniAudio-Token learns comprehensive universal representations while preserving high-fidelity speech generation. When integrated with downstream LLMs, it outperforms all single-codebook baseline tokenizers on both understanding and generation tasks, effectively serving as a unified audio interface. We publicly release all our code, including training and inference scripts, together with the model checkpoints at https://github.com/Tencent/Universal_Audio_Tokenizer.

</details>


### [221] [Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31545)
*Yilun Qiu,Xiaoyan Zhao,Yang Zhang,Yuxin Chen,Cilin Yan,Jiayin Cai,Xiaolong Jiang,Yao Hu,Yoko Yamakata,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PARL, a framework for personalized evaluation of LLMs by learning user-specific preferences from interaction histories, ensuring representativeness, consistency, and discriminativeness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods for LLMs lack the ability to capture subjective, user-specific preferences in long-term interactions, necessitating a more reliable and effective personalized evaluation approach.

Method: The paper proposes PARL, a framework that formulates personalized evaluation as a learning problem, inducing preference-aware rubrics from user histories and using a discriminative reinforcement learning objective to ensure alignment with user preferences.

Result: Experiments on real-world tasks demonstrate that PARL reliably identifies user-aligned responses, generalizes across users and tasks, and captures fine-grained evaluative patterns.

Conclusion: PARL addresses the limitations of existing evaluation methods by learning user-specific rubrics, offering a robust solution for personalized alignment in LLMs.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve from general-purpose assistants to user-centric agents, personalization has become central to aligning model behavior with individual preferences, making the evaluation of personalized alignment a critical bottleneck. Existing evaluation methods-ranging from automatic metrics to LLM-as-a-judge approaches-fail to capture subjective, user-specific preferences embedded in long-term interaction histories. We identify three essential principles for reliable and effective personalized evaluation: Representativeness, User-Consistency, and Discriminativeness. To address these principles, we introduce Personalized Evaluation as Learning, a paradigm that formulates personalized evaluation as a learning problem rather than a static judgment. Under this paradigm, we propose PARL (Preference-Aware Rubric Learning for Personalized Evaluation), a framework that learns to induce preference-aware evaluation rubrics directly from raw user histories and performs a self-validation mechanism to ensure consistency with the user's preferences. PARL integrates rubric induction with a discriminative reinforcement learning objective that contrasts user-authored responses against competitive personalized model outputs, enabling the learned rubrics to capture precise, user-specific decision boundaries. Experiments on real-world personalized text generation tasks show that PARL consistently induces high-fidelity rubrics that reliably identify user-aligned responses and generalize across users and tasks, while capturing stable stylistic preferences and fine-grained evaluative patterns. To ensure reproducibility, our code is available at https://github.com/SnowCharmQ/PARL.

</details>


### [222] [Semantic Triplet Restoration: A Novel Protocol for Hierarchical Table Understanding in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31550)
*Yibin Zhao,Fangxin Shang,Dingrui Yang,Yuqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: STR protocol simplifies table-QA by rewriting cells as semantic triplets, improving efficiency and reducing token usage, especially for smaller models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current table-QA pipelines rely on layout-oriented serializations like HTML/Markdown, which introduce overhead and complicate header-cell alignment inference.

Method: Proposed Semantic Triplet Restoration (STR) rewrites cells as <item path, feature path, value> triplets and uses TripletQL to route queries.

Result: STR matches or outperforms HTML-based baselines on four benchmarks while reducing input tokens, with greater benefits for smaller models and longer tables.

Conclusion: Explicit semantic representations like STR enhance efficiency in table-QA, particularly under constrained inference budgets.

Abstract: Table question answering requires models to recover semantic relations encoded implicitly by two-dimensional layout, merged cells, and hierarchical headers. Current pipelines typically use HTML or Markdown as intermediate table representations, but these layout-oriented serializations introduce markup overhead and require large language models to infer header-cell alignments from row and column spans. We propose Semantic Triplet Restoration (STR), a protocol that rewrites each cell as an atomic fact <item path, feature path, value>, where the item path specifies the row-wise entity, the feature path specifies the hierarchical attribute, and the value contains the cell content. We also present TripletQL, a lightweight query-aware router that uses STR to select an appropriate rendering or filtered subset of triplets for each question. Across four Chinese and English table-QA benchmarks, STR matches or improves upon HTML-based baselines while reducing input tokens. The relative benefit grows for smaller language models and longer table contexts, suggesting that explicit semantic representations are especially useful under constrained inference budgets. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Phoenix-ni/STR.git .

</details>


### [223] [What Am I Missing? Question-Answering as Hidden State Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31561)
*Chu Fei Luo,Samuel Dahan,Xiaodan Zhu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores test-time reasoning in LLMs using question-asking as an inference-time intervention. A student-teacher setup is used to train a probe on hidden states, revealing insights into self-diagnosis and the limitations of interventions due to model self-consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the mechanisms of chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs and improve reasoning through question-asking as an intervention.

Method: A student-teacher setup where a student asks questions, and a probe is trained on hidden states to predict correctness. A gating policy is defined to maximize correctness likelihood.

Result: The probe captures meaningful signals from self-diagnosis during question generation, but interventions are limited by model self-consistency, showing a gap between detection and recovery.

Conclusion: Question-asking interventions reveal challenges in self-refinement under uncertainty, highlighting the gap between diagnosis and correction in LLMs.

Abstract: Test-time reasoning has become a significant field of study since the introduction of chain-of-thought reasoning in large language models (LLMs). However, the mechanisms of this reasoning process are still under-explored -- from the same input prompt, and even the same partial solution, LLMs can produce varied answers if sampled multiple times. We propose to leverage question-asking as an inference-time intervention that articulates information about the model's hidden state. To achieve that, we present a student-teacher setting where a student asks questions to a teacher. We train a probe on the student's hidden state before and after asking a question and find it is predictive of the trajectory's final correctness, even before generating the teacher's answer. This suggests there is a meaningful signal from the self-diagnosis that occurs during question generation rather than information transfer from the teacher. We then frame question-asking as a sequential decision problem, using this probe as a quality score, and define a gating policy to ask questions that maximize likelihood of correctness. We find that the success of question-asking as an intervention is largely dependent on the model's self-consistency. Our empirical results show a gap between detection and recovery; while our gating policy captures model correctness and uncertainty, interventions are equally likely to harm correct trajectories as they are to recover incorrect ones. This gap between diagnosis and correction has broader implications on language models' capacity for self-refinement under uncertainty.

</details>


### [224] [Disagreeing Rationales: Rethinking Classification and Explainability Evaluation in Hate Speech Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31563)
*Benedetta Muscato,Beiduo Chen,Gizem Gezici,Barbara Plank,Fosca Giannotti*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores human rationale variation in NLP tasks, proposing a unified framework to evaluate labels and rationales using diverse models and metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of exploration in human rationale variation and the unclear methods for evaluating and aggregating rationales, especially in subjective tasks like hate speech detection.

Method: Unifies models, training strategies, loss functions, and metrics under a single protocol, evaluating classification and explainability metrics across different label and rationale representations.

Result: Hard and soft metrics favor softer rationale representations, showing their effectiveness in capturing variation and suggesting a need to rethink evaluation in subjective NLP.

Conclusion: The study highlights the importance of softer rationale representations in capturing human reasoning variation and calls for reevaluating evaluation methods in subjective NLP tasks.

Abstract: Human disagreement is ubiquitous and well-known in labeling. However, variation in explanations, captured through token-level human rationales, remains far less explored. At the same time, it is unclear how to best evaluate human labels and rationales -- or even how to best aggregate rationales beyond majority vote -- in light of this variation. Yet, rationales may provide additional insights into the richness of human reasoning, that may differ in style, values and interpretations -- especially in subjective NLP tasks like hate speech detection. In this work, we unify diverse models, training strategies, loss functions, and existing evaluation metrics under a single protocol by systematically re-implementing them across different label and rationale representation spaces. Classification metrics are organized around two key properties -- predictive and distributional -- while explainability metrics through three complementary dimensions: plausibility, faithfulness, and complexity. In this unified supervision framework, we evaluate model behavior across classification and explainability metrics, as well as metric sensitivity to the choice of label (hard and soft) and rationale representation space (hard, intermediate and soft). Results show that both hard and soft metrics favor softer representations, highlighting their effectiveness in capturing variation and the need to rethink evaluation in subjective NLP.

</details>


### [225] [What Gets Unmasked First? Trajectory Analysis of Diffusion Models for Graph-to-Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31564)
*Qing Wang,Jacob Devasier,Chengkai Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) for graph-to-text generation, revealing their unique token-unmasking order (entities first, structural last). It identifies a failure mode in supervised fine-tuning (SFT) that disrupts this order and proposes a solution (lambda-scaled structural decoding) to improve performance. A new model, Graph-LLaDA, incorporating graph structure, outperforms previous baselines in cross-dataset evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and improve the generation behavior of MDLMs for graph-to-text tasks, addressing issues like misprioritization of tokens during decoding caused by SFT and lack of explicit graph structure integration.

Method: Analyzes MDLM generation trajectories, identifies SFT failure modes, proposes lambda-scaled structural decoding, and introduces Graph-LLaDA, a model combining Graph Transformer with LLaDA's decoder.

Result: Lambda-scaled structural decoding recovers +9.4 BLEU-4. Graph-LLaDA generalizes better than previous baselines in cross-dataset evaluations.

Conclusion: MDLMs offer promising but distinct generation behavior for graph-to-text tasks. Addressing SFT disruptions and integrating graph structure improves performance and generalization.

Abstract: We present the first systematic study of masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) for graph-to-text generation. We analyze MDLM generation trajectories -- the order in which tokens are unmasked during iterative decoding -- and find that, unlike autoregressive LLMs which generate text linearly, MDLMs naturally prioritize entities first, followed by relational and function words, with structural tokens resolved last. We further identify a previously undocumented failure mode of supervised fine-tuning: SFT disrupts this strategy by prematurely anchoring structural sentence-ending tokens early in the decoding trajectory, effectively fixing the output length which can lead to omitted or hallucinated information. To address this, we propose lambda-scaled structural decoding, a training-free inference-time modification that downweights structural token confidence and recovers +9.4 BLEU-4. Finally, we introduce Graph-LLaDA, which integrates a Graph Transformer encoder into LLaDA's decoding process to explicitly incorporate relational graph structure. Cross-dataset evaluation on LAGRANGE reveals that previous baselines overfit to dataset-specific patterns, while LLM- and MDLM-based approaches generalize significantly better.

</details>


### [226] [LongTraceRL: Learning Long-Context Reasoning from Search Agent Trajectories with Rubric Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31584)
*Nianyi Lin,Jiajie Zhang,Lei Hou,Juanzi Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces 	extsc{LongTraceRL}, a method to improve long-context reasoning in language models by using tiered distractors and rubric rewards for fine-grained supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context reasoning is challenging for language models, which struggle to locate and integrate key information in extensive content. Existing reinforcement learning methods lack confusability in distractors and fine-grained reward signals.

Method: 	extsc{LongTraceRL} generates multi-hop questions via knowledge graph random walks and uses search agent trajectories to build tiered distractors. It also introduces a rubric reward for entity-level process supervision.

Result: Experiments on three reasoning LLMs across five benchmarks show that 	extsc{LongTraceRL} outperforms baselines and promotes evidence-grounded reasoning.

Conclusion: 	extsc{LongTraceRL} effectively addresses challenges in long-context reasoning by combining tiered distractors and fine-grained rubric rewards, improving model performance.

Abstract: Long-context reasoning remains a central challenge for large language models, which often fail to locate and integrate key information in extensive distracting content. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has shown promise for this task, yet existing methods are limited by low-confusability distractors and sparse, outcome-only reward signals that cannot supervise intermediate reasoning steps. To address these issues, we introduce \textsc{LongTraceRL}. For data construction, we generate multi-hop questions via knowledge graph random walks and leverage search agent trajectories to build \emph{tiered distractors}: documents the agent read but did not cite (high confusability) and documents that appeared in search results but were never opened (low confusability), producing training contexts that are far more challenging than those built by random sampling or one-shot search. For reward design, we propose a \emph{rubric reward} that uses the gold entities along each reasoning chain as fine-grained, entity-level process supervision. This rubric reward is applied only to responses with correct final answers (positive-only strategy), distinguishing the reasoning quality among correct responses and preventing reward hacking. Experiments on three reasoning LLMs (4B--30B) across five long-context benchmarks demonstrate that \textsc{LongTraceRL} consistently outperforms strong baselines and encourages comprehensive, evidence-grounded reasoning. Codes, datasets and models are available at \href{https://github.com/THU-KEG/LongTraceRL}{https://github.com/THU-KEG/LongTraceRL}.

</details>


### [227] [Language Models Learn Constructional Semantics, Not To Mention Syntax: Investigating LM Understanding of Paired-Focus Constructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31586)
*Wesley Scivetti,Ethan Wilcox,Nathan Schneider,Kanishka Misra,Leonie Weissweiler*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores whether open-source models can understand rare Paired-Focus constructions in English, finding that some modest-sized models succeed, while human-scale data-trained models fail. Learning dynamics reveal semantic understanding emerges later than syntactic knowledge and correlates with world knowledge gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if open-source models can robustly understand rare constructions like 'let alone' or 'much less,' and to uncover the learning dynamics behind this acquisition.

Method: Constructed a novel dataset to test Paired-Focus constructions using scalar adjectival semantics and world knowledge. Tested models of varying sizes, architectures, and pretraining data.

Result: Modestly sized models showed sensitivity to Paired-Focus forms and meanings, while human-scale data-trained models failed. Semantic understanding emerged later than syntactic knowledge and correlated with world knowledge gains.

Conclusion: Open-source models can grasp rare Paired-Focus constructions, with semantic understanding linked to broader world knowledge domains.

Abstract: Grasping the semantics of rare constructions (form-meaning pairings) has been shown to be a challenging problem that has currently only been solved by the largest LLMs. It remains an open question if open-source models have robust constructional understanding, and if so, what learning dynamics underlie the acquisition of this knowledge. Focusing on a set of rare Paired-Focus constructions in English (e.g. "let alone", "much less"), we construct a novel dataset to test their meanings using both scalar adjectival semantics and general world knowledge. Testing a wide range of models differing in parameter count, architecture, and pretraining dataset size, we find that several modestly sized models are sensitive to both the forms and the meanings of Paired-Focus constructions, though models trained on human-scale data fail at all meaning evaluations. Turning to training dynamics for a set of open-checkpoint models, we find that Paired-Focus understanding emerges later in training than Paired-Focus syntactic knowledge, and that learning of Paired-Focus semantics is correlated with gains in some domains of world knowledge. Overall, our empirical results support the conclusion that modestly sized open-source models can grasp the rare Paired-Focus constructions, and demonstrate a connection between knowledge of Paired-Focus constructions and other meaning domains.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [228] [Gait2Hip-60: A Unified Deep Learning Benchmark for Predicting Hip Muscle Forces and Joint Moments from Multi-Cadence Gait Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30374)
*Jiaqi Zhang,Ji Hou,Qing Sun,Xianzhi Gao,Bo Huo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A deep learning framework (Transformer model) effectively predicts hip muscle forces and joint moments from gait kinematics, outperforming LSTM and Mamba in healthy subjects and showing moderate accuracy in patients with osteonecrosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional musculoskeletal simulation for estimating hip dynamics during gait is time-consuming and clinically impractical. This study aims to develop a deep learning solution for direct prediction from gait kinematics.

Method: Gait data from 60 healthy adults were collected under three cadence conditions. Joint angles were input, and OpenSim-derived metrics served as outputs. Three models (LSTM, Transformer, Mamba) were trained and evaluated, with the best model tested on a cohort of 9 ONFH patients without retraining.

Result: Transformer achieved the best performance in healthy subjects (R2 = 0.819 for muscle force, 0.862 for joint moment) and retained moderate accuracy in ONFH patients (R2 = 0.537 for muscle force, 0.569 for joint moment).

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the feasibility of predicting hip dynamics from gait kinematics using deep learning, with Transformer as a strong baseline. Clinical application requires further validation and improved generalization.

Abstract: Estimating hip muscle forces and joint moments during gait typically relies on musculoskeletal simulation, which is informative but time-consuming and difficult to apply in clinical settings. This study developed a deep learning framework to predict these hip dynamics parameters directly from lower-limb gait kinematics and compared three representative sequence models under a unified protocol. Gait data were collected from 60 healthy adults under three metronome-guided cadence conditions. Ten bilateral lower-limb joint angles were used as inputs, and OpenSim-derived hip muscle forces and hip joint moments were used as reference outputs. Three deep learning models of LSTM, Transformer, and Mamba were trained and evaluated using the same subject-level split, preprocessing pipeline, and metrics. The best model was then directly tested on an external cohort of 9 patients with osteonecrosis of the femoral head (ONFH) without retraining. In the healthy-subject benchmark, Transformer achieved the best subject-level mean performance for both hip muscle force prediction (RMSE = 1.33 N/kg, MAE = 0.57 N/kg, R2 = 0.819) and hip joint moment prediction (RMSE = 0.11 Nm/kg, MAE = 0.07 Nm/kg, R2 = 0.862), with similar advantages across walking cadences. In zero-shot external validation, Transformer retained moderate predictive ability in ONFH for hip muscle force prediction (RMSE = 1.51 N/kg, MAE = 0.70 N/kg, R2 = 0.537) and hip joint moment prediction (RMSE = 0.17 Nm/kg, MAE = 0.12 Nm/kg, R2 = 0.569). These findings support the feasibility of estimating hip dynamics from gait kinematics, identify Transformer as a strong baseline, and highlight the need for broader pathological validation and improved generalization before clinical application.

</details>


### [229] [Unicorn: Scaling High-Dimensional Time Series Forecasting via Universal Correlation Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30376)
*Haochen Yuan,Yichen Song,Yunbo Wang,Xiaokang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unicorn introduces a scalable, multi-dataset pretraining framework for high-dimensional time series, addressing the trade-off between channel-independent and channel-dependent models by decoupling correlation modeling from channel identities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between channel-independent models (scalable but ignore inter-channel dependencies) and channel-dependent models (expressive but dimension-bounded) for handling heterogeneous time series data.

Method: Unicorn uses a latent prototype codebook to project channels into a shared latent space, learning identity-agnostic, reusable interaction patterns for transfer across domains.

Result: Unicorn outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, especially in few-shot transfer scenarios, demonstrating scalability and generalization.

Conclusion: Unicorn provides a scalable solution for multivariate time series foundation models, enabling effective transfer learning across diverse datasets.

Abstract: Modern time series architectures face a fundamental trade-off: channel-independent models scale well with increasing data volume but ignore critical inter-channel dependencies, while channel-dependent models are expressive but remain ``dimension-bounded'', struggling to generalize across heterogeneous datasets.To bridge this gap, we introduce Unicorn (Universal Correlation Network), a framework for scalable, multi-dataset pretraining on high-dimensional time series. At the core of Unicorn is a latent prototype codebook that decouples correlation modeling from specific channel identities. By projecting heterogeneous channels into a shared latent space, UniCorN learns identity-agnostic, reusable interaction patterns that transfer across domains with diverse dimensionalities and semantics. Extensive experiments show that Unicorn significantly outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting architectures, particularly in few-shot transfer scenarios, offering a scalable path toward multivariate time series foundation models.

</details>


### [230] [When LLMs Learn to Be Consistently Wrong: A Multi-Model Study of Linear Representations of Synthetic Deception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30381)
*Vahideh Zolfaghari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies synthetic dishonesty in AI models, showing that deceptive alignment can be detected early in training, with Gemma-2 models exhibiting exceptional robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding deceptive alignment in AI models is critical for AI safety, with synthetic dishonesty serving as a controlled testbed.

Method: Multi-model paradigm with honest and deceptive variants of five transformer models fine-tuned using LoRA, analyzed via linear probes and mechanistic tests.

Result: Linear probes detect dishonesty with near-perfect AUC early in training. Gemma-2 models show high-dimensional stability, while others exhibit representational collapse.

Conclusion: Dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched, with implications for activation-based monitoring in AI safety.

Abstract: Deceptive alignment, in which models maintain accurate internal representations while deliberately producing false outputs, remains a central challenge in AI safety. While strategic deception is the primary long-term concern, synthetic dishonesty - induced via direct optimization on incorrect answers - provides a controlled testbed for studying the representational basis of learned deception. We introduce a multi-model paradigm in which honest and deceptive variants of five transformer models (Pythia-1.4B, Gemma-2-2B/9B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) are fine-tuned using LoRA on the same question distribution. Linear probes trained on mean-pooled hidden states detect synthetic dishonesty with near-perfect AUC (greater than or equal to 0.99) as early as layers 1-3 in four architectures, while Pythia-1.4B reaches a peak of 0.705. Logistic regression probes consistently match or outperform MLP probes, supporting the Linear Representation Hypothesis. Probes trained on TruthfulQA generalize with near-zero loss (Delta AUC approx. 0) to held-out MMLU subjects. Late-layer representations show strong robustness to Gaussian noise, with Gemma-2 models exhibiting exceptional stability. Mechanistic analysis of Fisher Discriminant Ratio, effective rank, centroid geometry, directional stability, cross-domain alignment, and calibration (ECE) reveals two regimes: representational collapse in Pythia/Llama/Qwen versus high-dimensional preservation in Gemma-2. Across all models, the dishonesty direction consolidates progressively in deeper layers, with optimal calibration (ECE less than 0.01 except Pythia) achievable in layers 1-4. These results demonstrate that robust, domain-invariant dishonesty representations can be rapidly entrenched via modest supervised fine-tuning, with implications for activation-based monitoring.

</details>


### [231] [LongDS-Bench: On the Failure of Long-Horizon Agentic Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30434)
*Kewei Xu,Xiaoben Lu,Shuofei Qiao,Zihan Ding,Haoming Xu,Lei Liang,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces LongDS, a benchmark for long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis, testing agents' ability to track evolving analytical contexts. Results show current models struggle with maintaining correct analytical states, especially over long interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for data analysis evaluate isolated or short interactive tasks, failing to test agents' ability to handle long-horizon, evolving analytical contexts. This gap limits the reliability of agents in real-world data analysis scenarios.

Method: The authors created LongDS, a benchmark with 68 tasks derived from real-world Kaggle notebooks across six domains. Tasks involve maintaining, updating, restoring, and composing analytical states with an average dependency span of 11.3 turns. Five state-of-the-art models were evaluated on this benchmark.

Result: The best model achieved only 48.45% accuracy, with performance dropping 47 points from early to late turns. Long-horizon errors accounted for 52%--69% of failures. Adding more agent steps did not significantly improve performance, indicating the bottleneck lies in maintaining correct analytical states.

Conclusion: LongDS highlights the challenges of long-horizon data analysis and the need for better methods to maintain analytical states. The benchmark is released to support further research in this area.

Abstract: Real-world data analysis is inherently iterative, yet existing benchmarks mostly evaluate isolated or short interactive tasks, leaving agents' ability to track evolving analytical context over long horizons untested. We introduce LongDS, a benchmark for long-horizon, multi-turn data analysis where agents must maintain, update, restore, and compose evolving analytical states. LongDS comprises 68 tasks constructed from real-world Kaggle notebooks, spanning 2,225 turns across six domains including Geoscience, Business, and Education. Tasks are designed around state-evolution patterns (e.g., counterfactual perturbation, rollback, multi-state composition), with an average dependency span of 11.3 turns. Evaluating five state-of-the-art models, we find that the best model reaches only 48.45% average accuracy, performance drops nearly 47 points from early to late turns, and long-horizon errors account for 52%--69% of failures. Further analysis shows that additional agent steps do not necessarily improve performance, suggesting that the key bottleneck is maintaining a correct analytical state rather than increasing interaction budget. We release LongDS to support research on reliable long-horizon agentic data analysis. Code and data will be released at https://github.com/zjunlp/DataMind.

</details>


### [232] [LLMs Without Deep Neural Networks: New Architecture, Benefits and Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30385)
*Vincent Granville*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new model replaces DNNs with a closed-form solution for global loss optimization, eliminating training steps, while matching RBF network benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for explainable and accurate alternatives to DNNs, inspired by recent interest in RBF networks.

Method: Proposes a model that finds the global optimum of the loss function in closed form without iterative training.

Result: The model achieves comparable benefits to RBF networks (explainability, accuracy) without DNNs.

Conclusion: The new model is a viable, efficient alternative to DNNs, validated by case studies and comparisons.

Abstract: The purpose of this article is to provide validation to my deep neural network alternative in the context of LLMs. Very recently, there has been a significant interest by Chinese researchers in a model called RBF network, as a substitute to standard DNNs, with increased explainability and higher accuracy. It turns out that my new model, discovered independently, is based on the exact same machinery. But with a major twist: it does not need DNN as it finds the global optimum of the loss function in closed form, in one iteration, thus eliminating the tedious training step. Here I provide a high-level overview of my technology, with case study and comparison to similar methods.

</details>


### [233] [Functional MRI Time Series Generation via Wavelet-Based Image Transform and Spectral Flow Matching for Brain Disorder Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30387)
*Hwa Hui Tew,Junn Yong Loo,Fang Yu Leong,Julia K. Lau,Ding Fan,Hernando Ombao,Raphaël C. -W. Phan,Chee Pin Tan,Chee-Ming Ting*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSFM is a novel fMRI generative framework using dual frequency representation and spectral flow matching to synthesize realistic BOLD signals, improving brain network classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The resource-intensive nature of fMRI acquisition limits high-fidelity samples, and existing generative models struggle with replicating the non-stationarity and dynamics of BOLD signals.

Method: DSFM uses discrete wavelet and cosine transforms to capture multi-scale variations and localized energy compaction, followed by spectral flow matching to generate BOLD signals.

Result: The framework generates physiologically plausible BOLD signals, enhancing downstream fMRI-based brain network classification.

Conclusion: DSFM effectively addresses the challenges of fMRI data synthesis, offering improved fidelity and utility for brain analysis models.

Abstract: Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) provides non-invasive access to dynamic brain activity by measuring blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signals over time. However, the resource-intensive nature of fMRI acquisition limits the availability of high-fidelity samples required for data-driven brain analysis models. While modern generative models can synthesize fMRI data, they often remain challenging in replicating their inherent non-stationarity, intricate spatiotemporal dynamics, and physiological variations of raw BOLD signals. To address these challenges, we propose Dual-Spectral Flow Matching (DSFM), a novel fMRI generative framework that cascades dual frequency representation of BOLD signals with spectral flow matching. Specifically, our framework first converts BOLD signals into a wavelet decomposition map via a discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to capture globalized transient and multi-scale variations, and projects into the discrete cosine transform (DCT) space across brain regions and time to exploit localized energy compaction of low-frequency dominant BOLD coefficients. Subsequently, a spectral flow matching model is trained to generate class-conditioned cosine-frequency representation. The generated samples are reconstructed through inverse DCT and inverse DWT operations to recover physiologically plausible time-domain BOLD signals. This dual-transform approach imposes structured frequency priors and preserves key physiological brain dynamics. Ultimately, we demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through improved downstream fMRI-based brain network classification. The code is available at https://github.com/htew0001/DSFM.git .

</details>


### [234] [A Novel Evaluation Metric for Unsupervised Learning in AIS-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection: MADQI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30388)
*Ismet Gocer,Zakirul Bhuiyan,Raza Hasan,Shakeel Ahmad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a systematic framework (MADQI) for evaluating unsupervised anomaly detection in maritime AIS data, achieving an 80.37% score.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of systematic evaluation measures for unsupervised learning in detecting maritime anomalies like abnormal vessel behaviors.

Method: Develops MADQI, a composite index with four metrics (ARC, PPS, SDS, ECE), using Haversine distance and adaptive scaling for anomaly detection.

Result: Achieved MADQI score of 80.37%, with strong performance in identifying extreme anomalies (ECE: 0.907) and consistency (ARC: 1.000).

Conclusion: The framework is effective and reliable for evaluating unsupervised anomaly detection in maritime AIS data.

Abstract: This paper introduces a new systematic framework for detecting anomalies in maritime Automatic Identification System (AIS) datasets. These anomalies include abnormal vessel behaviours related to speed, position jumps, time gaps, and turn angles. Although unsupervised learning algorithms such as Isolation Forest are widely used for detecting anomalous vessel movements, they often lack systematic and meaningful evaluation measures. To address this limitation, we propose a novel quality metric called Maritime Anomaly Detection Quality Index (MADQI). The prosed MADQI is a composite index designed to evaluate the anomaly detection performance of machine learning models without requiring labelled data. The proposed framework uses Haversine distance calculations to analyse AIS datasets and identify anomalies based on their spatial and behavioural characteristics. The proposed MADQI evaluation framework integrates four interconnected metrics: Anomaly Rate Consistency (ARC), Physical Plausibility Score (PPS), Score Distribution Separation (SDS), and Extreme Case Evidence (ECE). These metrics are combined through automatic normalisation using multi-chunk evaluation and adaptive scaling techniques. Experimental results on the AIS dataset show that the proposed framework achieved a MADQI score of 80.37%, demonstrating its effectiveness for unsupervised anomaly detection. In particular, the algorithm performed strongly in identifying abnormal vessel behaviour. Among the individual MADQI components, ECE and ARC achieved scores of 0.907 and 1.000, respectively, indicating excellent capability in detecting extreme anomalies and maintaining anomaly rate consistency. Overall, these results are encouraging and demonstrate that the proposed framework provides a reliable and meaningful approach for evaluating unsupervised anomaly detection in maritime AIS data.

</details>


### [235] [NumLeak: Public Numeric Benchmarks as Latent Labels in Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30393)
*Anany Kotawala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces NumLeak, a framework to measure memorized recall in LLMs, showing top-tier models recall public numeric benchmarks with high fidelity, while a simple system-prompt defense blocks most attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the issue of public numeric benchmarks being memorized by LLMs, leading to misleading evaluations that conflate memorization with genuine skill.

Method: Uses API-boundary probes and white-box validation on an open causal LM to measure recall fidelity and detect memorization, including logprob ranking and regression analysis.

Result: Top LLMs recall benchmarks like Fama-French returns with high accuracy (r=0.97-0.99). Memorization is confirmed via logprob ranking, and a simple defense blocks 99.8% of attacks.

Conclusion: Memorization of public benchmarks is widespread in LLMs, and simple defenses can mitigate it, though closed-API probes understate the issue.

Abstract: Public numeric benchmarks appear in pretraining, so an evaluation that conditions on a date may be measuring memorized recall rather than out-of-sample skill. We introduce NumLeak, a measurement framework that combines API-boundary probes on production models with a white-box controlled validation on an open causal LM. Top-tier frontier LLMs recall the Fama-French market excess return at 3-seed pooled Pearson r=0.97-0.99 while staying within 0.15 within-25bps on the five sibling factors; comparable fidelity appears on U.S. unemployment, CPI inflation, and NOAA temperature. On a recent-release holdout, parse rate collapses to 21-57% but r stays at approximately 0.99 on months answered, the refuse-or-recall asymmetry a memorized channel predicts. The white-box experiment reproduces the dose-response, and logprob ranking detects memorization that open-ended generation misses, implying closed-API black-box probes understate the channel. A Sonnet "date to market-sentiment" regression that correlates with true Mkt-RF at r=0.74 collapses to r=0.02 once the model's own recall is residualized out. A one-line system-prompt defense blocks 99.8% of a non-adaptive single-turn suffix attack set at near-zero utility cost on conceptual and historical-narrative queries

</details>


### [236] [Generalized Intention Modeling in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.31318)
*Mateusz Odrowaz-Sypniewski,Jasmine Bayrooti,Ajay Shankar,Amanda Prorok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A task-adaptive opponent modeling framework is introduced to learn intent representations for better decision-making in multi-agent reinforcement learning by maximizing mutual information with the ego-agent's future returns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing opponent modeling methods assume fixed intent representations, which may not universally apply. Intentions are task- and environment-dependent, requiring a more adaptive approach.

Method: The proposed framework learns a performance-driven mixture of multiple intent representations, including a new representation that maximizes mutual information with the ego-agent's future returns.

Result: The approach consistently matches or outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse tasks and provides insights into the effectiveness of different opponent modeling strategies.

Conclusion: The task-adaptive framework improves performance by capturing relevant opponent information, offering a flexible and effective solution for intent modeling in multi-agent environments.

Abstract: Modeling an opponent's intent is critical for effective decision-making in non-cooperative, competitive, and general-sum multi-agent reinforcement learning. Existing opponent modeling methods encode intent using an embedding derived from episode information chosen a priori, such as the opponent's next action or a future environment state, and use this to guide the ego-agent's behavior. These approaches assume that the chosen information is universally representative of intent; however, we show empirically that this is not the case as intentions are often task- and environment-dependent. To address this, we introduce a task-adaptive opponent modeling framework that learns a performance-driven mixture of multiple intent representations. We further introduce a new intention representation that maximizes mutual information with the ego-agent's future returns, thereby capturing opponent information that is most directly relevant to performance. Our approach consistently matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art baselines across diverse tasks and yields insights into when and why different opponent modeling strategies succeed.

</details>


### [237] [Calibrated Preference Learning: The Case of Label Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30447)
*Santo M. A. R. Thies,Viktor Bengs,Timo Kaufmann,Sebastian J. Vollmer,Eyke Hüllermeier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper formalizes calibration for probabilistic label ranking, introducing a hierarchy of notions (full, sub-, top-k rankings) and shows their relationships. It finds existing models often poorly calibrated and highlights calibration's importance beyond top-1 accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibration is crucial for reliable decision-making but hasn't been formally addressed for probabilistic label ranking, where structure and modalities like pairwise or top-k predictions matter.

Method: The authors formalize calibration for label ranking, develop a hierarchy of notions (full, sub-, top-k rankings), prove their interrelationships, and empirically evaluate popular models for calibration.

Result: Popular label ranking models are often poorly calibrated, with significant differences between sub-ranking and top-k metrics. Calibration correlates with benchmark accuracy but captures additional quality dimensions.

Conclusion: The findings underscore the importance of calibration in label ranking, motivating further research into miscalibration's downstream effects and methods to improve it.

Abstract: Calibration, the alignment of predicted probabilities with true outcome frequencies, is essential for reliable decision-making. While extensively studied for classification and regression, calibration has not been formally addressed for probabilistic label ranking, where the goal is to predict a distribution over orderings of a label set. Naively treating rankings as classes ignores their structure and fails to capture important modalities such as pairwise and top-k predictions. We formalize calibration for label ranking and develop a hierarchy of notions covering full rankings, sub-rankings, and top-k rankings. We prove that full-rank calibration implies the others but not conversely, and sub-ranking and top-k calibration are incomparable. Empirically, we find popular label ranking models are often poorly calibrated, with substantial differences between sub-ranking and top-k metrics. Applying our framework to RLHF reward models, we find that calibration correlates strongly but not perfectly with benchmark accuracy, suggesting it captures a meaningful quality dimension beyond top-1 accuracy. These findings motivate future work on understanding the downstream effects of miscalibration and developing methods to correct it.

</details>


### [238] [Bounded Behavioral Indistinguishability for Black-Box LLM Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30448)
*Munawar Hasan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces bounded behavioral indistinguishability to evaluate black-box LLM distillation, showing that output similarity alone is insufficient and adversarial evaluation reveals remaining behavioral differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation of evaluating LLM distillation solely based on output similarity, the authors propose a more rigorous framework to ensure behavioral indistinguishability from the teacher model.

Method: The authors formalize bounded behavioral indistinguishability and test it on Qwen and Llama teacher-student pairs using a 5,000-prompt behavioral probe suite, comparing base and LoRA-distilled students.

Result: LoRA improves semantic similarity but adversarial evaluation shows residual differences. Discriminators retain some advantage, and artifacts persist in style, robustness, and domain-technical prompts. Distillation reduces distinguishability but does not eliminate it.

Conclusion: Semantic fidelity is useful but insufficient for black-box LLM distillation evaluation. The paper advocates for bounded, adversarial, and category-aware evaluation to ensure true behavioral indistinguishability.

Abstract: Black-box LLM distillation is usually evaluated as an output-matching problem: a student is considered successful when its responses are semantically similar to, or task-consistent with, those of a teacher. However, output similarity does not imply that the student is behaviorally indistinguishable from the model it imitates. We introduce bounded behavioral indistinguishability, formalized as $(ε,q,t,\mathbb{A})$-behavioral indistinguishability over an explicit prompt distribution, where $ε$ bounds distinguishing advantage, $q$ bounds oracle queries, $t$ bounds computation, and $\mathbb{A}$ denotes the adversary class.
  We instantiate this notion on Qwen and Llama teacher-student pairs using a controlled $5,000$-prompt behavioral probe suite. For each family, we compare the teacher with both the base student and the LoRA-distilled student, measuring whether distillation reduces distinguishability rather than merely improving similarity. LoRA raises semantic similarity from $0.788$ to $0.862$ for Qwen and from $0.814$ to $0.874$ for Llama. Yet adversarial evaluation reveals remaining behavioral differences: learned discriminators retain nonzero advantage, and pairwise category analysis shows artifacts concentrated in style/format, robustness, and domain-technical prompts. A pairwise teacher-identification adversary confirms this trend. With a different-family Llama judge and A/B-swap consistency filtering, Qwen distinguishing advantage drops from $0.158$ for the base student to $0.081$ after LoRA distillation. Query-budget experiments show that disagreement-guided acquisition does not consistently outperform stratified random sampling, indicating that coverage and diversity remain strong baselines. Our results show that semantic fidelity is useful but insufficient: black-box LLM distillation requires bounded, adversarial, and category-aware evaluation.

</details>


### [239] [VeriGate: Verifier-Gated Step-Level Supervision for GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30451)
*Aakriti Agrawal,Minghui Liu,Furong Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VeriGate enhances GRPO by addressing sparsity in verifier rewards and step-level credit assignment, improving reasoning model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO's sparse supervision and lack of step-level credit assignment limit learning and exploration. VeriGate aims to resolve these issues.

Method: VeriGate uses verifier-gated step-level supervision, future-cumulated rewards, and group-normalized token-level advantages.

Result: VeriGate improves accuracy by 20% and 12% for 1.5B and 7B models, reduces zero-gradient failures, and mitigates reward hacking.

Conclusion: VeriGate outperforms GRPO and PRM baselines, enhancing reasoning quality and robustness.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is an effective recipe for training reasoning models with verifier-based outcome rewards, but its supervision is sparse: when all sampled trajectories for a prompt receive the same verifier reward, the group-relative advantage collapses to zero and learning stalls. Outcome-only rewards also provide no step-level credit assignment, limiting exploration and making it harder to learn robust reasoning. We present VeriGate (Verifier-Gated Step-Level GRPO), a verifier-gated extension of GRPO that addresses these limitations with three design choices. First, VeriGate keeps the verifier in charge whenever verifier rewards induce a meaningful preference among sampled trajectories, and uses process supervision only when verifier rewards are degenerate. Second, instead of collapsing Process Reward Model (PRM) step scores into a single trajectory reward, VeriGate converts them into future-cumulated rewards to assign continuation-aware credit. Third, VeriGate transforms these rewards into group-normalized token-level advantages, restoring informative gradients and fine-grained credit assignment while remaining less susceptible to reward hacking than methods that optimize aggregated PRM scores. Empirically, training on MATH with 1.5B and 7B Qwen2.5-Instruct models and evaluating on six reasoning benchmarks, VeriGate improves average accuracy by about 20% and 12% for 1.5B and 7B models respectively, substantially reduces zero-gradient failures, decreases reward-hacking behavior, and improves reasoning quality relative to outcome-only GRPO and PRM-as-outcome baselines.

</details>


### [240] [A Unified Framework for Gradient Aggregation in Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30452)
*Zeou Hu,Kelvin Ho,Yaoliang Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unifying framework for gradient aggregation in multi-objective optimization (MOO) is proposed, establishing optimal convergence rates and introducing a sufficient alignment condition for Pareto stationarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MOO methods are fragmented with varying motivations and algorithms. This work aims to unify them under a single framework, providing theoretical guarantees and practical insights.

Method: Develops a framework for gradient aggregation in MOO, introducing a sufficient alignment condition and ensuring convergence through projection onto the dual cone. Also presents a primal optimization perspective to unify existing algorithms and introduces new variants like capped MGDA.

Result: The framework establishes optimal convergence rates and shows that non-conflicting gradient directions within the convex hull are sufficient for convergence. Capped MGDA demonstrates robustness in adversarial federated learning.

Conclusion: The work provides a cohesive theoretical foundation for MOO gradient aggregation, enabling better understanding, unification, and innovation in existing and new methods, validated through experiments.

Abstract: Many machine learning problems involve multiple inherent trade-offs that are best addressed by gradient-based multi-objective optimization (MOO) algorithms. Existing methods are often proposed with various motivations, analyzed case by case, and differ algorithmically in how the component gradients are aggregated at each step. In this work, we develop a unifying framework for gradient aggregation in MOO, establishing (optimal) rates of convergence to Pareto stationarity, the standard measure of performance in MOO. Central to our analysis is a sufficient alignment condition, from which we derive a theorem showing that non-conflicting directions, when chosen within the convex hull of gradients, form a fundamental sufficient condition for convergence. We further show that feasibility can be ensured through projection onto the dual cone, broadening the scope of methods that admit convergence guarantees. In parallel, we present a primal optimization perspective of gradient aggregation that encompasses established algorithms, clarifies their theoretical relationships, and enables the design of new variants. As an illustration, we introduce capped MGDA, derived from a CVaR-based formulation, and demonstrate its robustness in adversarial federated learning. Finally, we validate our theory through experiments on synthetic problems and practical benchmarks.

</details>


### [241] [DisjunctiveNet: Neural Symbolic Learning via Differentiable Convexified Optimization Layers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30456)
*Shraman Pal,Can Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a unified framework for enforcing hard, input-dependent mixed integer linear constraints in neural networks using hierarchical convex relaxations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses limitations in sparse datasets and leverages domain knowledge expressed as logical rules or inequalities, overcoming challenges in existing neuro-symbolic methods.

Method: Represents rules as disjunctive constraints and applies hierarchical convex relaxations to achieve tractable linear constraints embedded as differentiable optimization layers.

Result: Demonstrates perfect rule satisfaction and strong predictive performance on real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The framework effectively enforces hard constraints and improves model performance, bridging the gap between data-driven and knowledge-based approaches.

Abstract: Many learning tasks in science and engineering are characterized by sparse datasets, which limits the effectiveness of purely data-driven approaches. At the same time, these problems are often accompanied by rich domain knowledge derived from physical laws, operational requirements, and expert heuristics. Such knowledge is frequently expressed as rules involving logical propositions and linear inequalities. Existing neuro-symbolic methods typically enforce these rules approximately through soft penalties, assume input-independent rules when designing specialized architectures, or rely on non-differentiable post-processing at inference time to achieve hard constraint satisfaction. While recent advances in differentiable optimization layers enable end-to-end feasibility enforcement within neural networks, extending these approaches to logical or mixed-integer rules remains challenging due to inherent nonconvexity. In this work, we propose a unified end-to-end framework for enforcing hard, input-dependent mixed integer linear constraints within neural networks. Our approach represents rules as disjunctive constraints and applies hierarchical convex relaxations to obtain convex hull formulations. These relaxations yield tractable linear constraints that can be embedded as differentiable optimization layers while enabling exact rule satisfaction. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework on real-world datasets, achieving perfect rule satisfaction and strong predictive performance.

</details>


### [242] [Scalable Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning via State Augmentation and Consensus for Separable Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30461)
*Santiago Amaya-Corredor,Miguel Calvo-Fullana,Anders Jonsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distributed MARL method using state-augmented policy learning and dual variable consensus ensures scalable, feasible solutions for global constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Independent learning fails in systems with global constraints, necessitating a scalable, distributed approach for coordination.

Method: Agents learn augmented policies offline, conditioned on local state and dual variables, and achieve consensus on dual variables via local communication.

Result: The method scales linearly, ensures bounded constraint violations, and outperforms CTDE baselines in feasibility and scalability.

Conclusion: Consensus coordination is crucial for feasible solutions, enabling scalability to thousands of agents while satisfying constraints.

Abstract: We present a distributed approach for constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) that combines state-augmented policy learning with distributed consensus over dual variables. Our method targets systems where agents have separable dynamics but must coordinate to satisfy global resource constraints, a setting in which, as we demonstrate empirically, independent learning fails to produce feasible solutions because agents cannot determine appropriate individual contributions toward collective constraint satisfaction. The key technical contribution is showing that lightweight neighbor-to-neighbor consensus over Lagrange multipliers suffices for globally coordinated constraint enforcement while preserving the scalability of independent training. Each agent learns a single augmented policy offline, conditioned on both its local state and a dual variable encoding constraint feedback. During execution, agents reach agreement on this dual variable through local communication alone. We prove that under mild connectivity assumptions, the consensus error among agents' multipliers is bounded, and show that this translates to a bounded constraint violation that decreases with graph connectivity and the number of consensus rounds. Unlike centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) approaches, whose complexity grows at least quadratically with agent count, our method scales linearly in both training and execution. Experiments on smart grid demand response demonstrate that consensus coordination is \emph{essential for feasibility}: without it, agents satisfy grid capacity constraints only by indefinitely postponing demand, a degenerate non-solution. With consensus, agents converge to a shared dual variable and satisfy both grid constraints and demand fulfillment, scaling to thousands of agents while CTDE baselines are limited to dozens.

</details>


### [243] [idSCD: Identifying Training Datasets through Semantic Correlation Descriptors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30462)
*Andrada Gobeaja,Ionut Hodoroaga,Elena Burceanu,Marius Leordeanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores whether datasets can be identified by the spurious correlations they create during model training. It introduces semantic correlation descriptors (SCDs) for dataset-level membership inference, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to uncover dataset-specific traces in models' learned semantic correlations, moving beyond reliance on behavioral or distributional evidence like confidence scores or losses.

Method: The authors propose a white-box semantic fingerprinting approach using SCDs, which capture and compare semantic correlation structures across datasets. They also introduce a practical SCD-based membership score for inference without requiring leave-one-dataset-out models.

Result: SCDs perfectly separate matching and non-matching dataset pairs in a controlled setting. The SCD-based membership score outperforms black-box baselines (RMIA, Attack-P, LiRA) and a white-box baseline (SIF), with the highest performance and lowest std across diverse experimental settings.

Conclusion: Dataset membership can be effectively traced through internal semantic correlations, with significant performance gains, especially when datasets exhibit distinct semantic particularities.

Abstract: Can a dataset be recognized from the spurious correlations it induces during training? We argue that datasets leave dataset-specific traces in a model's learned semantic correlation structure: incidental regularities that are predictive within a dataset, but not causal for the underlying task, can be internalized during training. We use this insight to study dataset-level membership inference, moving beyond existing methods that rely on behavioral or distributional evidence such as confidence scores, losses, margins, generated samples, or query responses. We introduce a white-box semantic fingerprinting approach based on semantic correlation descriptors (SCDs), which capture the semantic correlation structure learned by a model and make it comparable across dataset mixtures. In a controlled leave-one-dataset-out diagnostic, SCDs recover dataset-specific changes and perfectly separate matching from non-matching dataset pairs. We then propose a practical SCD-based membership score that tests whether a target dataset is part of a model's training mixture using only the model's SCD and the target dataset's standalone SCD, without requiring leave-one-dataset-out models. Across three diverse experimental settings, with dataset groups for natural language inference, emotion classification, and medical text classification, we test both the advantages and limitations of SCD-based membership inference with different degrees of semantic separation and keyword support between dataset splits. On average, the classifier based on this score achieves the highest performance and the lowest std, outperforming black-box baselines RMIA, Attack-P, and LiRA, as well as the white-box SIF baseline. These results show that dataset membership can be traced through internal semantic correlations, with the largest relative gain exceeding 60% in ROC-AUC when dataset groups expose distinct semantic particularities.

</details>


### [244] [Can Subgraph Explanations Be Weaponized to Steal Graph Neural Networks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30470)
*Ojas Nimase,Jiate Li,Yue Zhao,Yushun Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a model extraction attack for graph classification under black-box constraints, using explanation outputs to guide edge sensitivity estimation and exploit explanation subgraphs, revealing vulnerabilities in GMLaaS platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to highlight exploitable vulnerabilities in Graph Machine Learning as a Service (GMLaaS) platforms, which implement explainability interfaces for transparency but inadvertently create attack surfaces for model extraction.

Method: The method involves using model explanation outputs to guide Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation with Hoeffding guarantees and exploiting explanation subgraphs to narrow the search space for decision boundaries.

Result: Extensive experiments on benchmark graph datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method over baselines, showcasing successful model extraction under strict black-box constraints.

Conclusion: The findings reveal that explainability interfaces in GMLaaS platforms can be exploited, emphasizing the need for defensive mechanisms and policy frameworks to address such vulnerabilities in explainable AI.

Abstract: Graph Machine Learning as a Service (GMLaaS) platforms increasingly implement explainability interfaces to meet regulatory transparency requirements. However, this transparency creates exploitable vulnerabilities for model extraction attacks. We present the first model extraction attack specifically designed for graph classification under strict black-box constraints where the attacker observes only discrete class labels and binary explanation masks (no probability scores, gradients, or confidence values). Our method (1) uses model explanation outputs to guide Monte Carlo edge sensitivity estimation toward decision boundaries, with Hoeffding concentration guarantees on estimation accuracy and (2) exploits explanation subgraphs to efficiently narrow the boundary search space. Extensive experiments on benchmark graph datasets across multiple domains demonstrate our method's superiority over comparable baselines. These findings demonstrate that such explainability interfaces create exploitable attack surfaces, informing both defensive mechanisms and policy frameworks for explainable AI mandates. The implementation code is provided in https://github.com/LabRAI/XSTEAL/.

</details>


### [245] [Universal Multiclass Transductive Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30479)
*Steve Hanneke,Hongao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores universal transductive online classification with an unbounded label space, characterizing learnability via two optimal rates and introducing the LCLL tree for analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of online learning with unbounded label spaces and pre-known instance sequences, aiming to characterize learnability and identify optimal rates.

Method: Introduces the Level-Constrained-Littlestone-Littlestone (LCLL) tree and leverages the indifference property to characterize learnability. Extends results to agnostic and stochastic cases.

Result: Shows that learnable classes have two optimal rates: bounded or logarithmically increasing. The LCLL tree and indifference property are key to this characterization.

Conclusion: The study successfully characterizes learnability in universal transductive online classification, providing new combinatorial tools (LCLL tree) and extending findings to broader scenarios.

Abstract: We consider the problem of universal transductive online classification with a possibly unbounded label space. This setting considers online learning, with the sequence of instances (without labels) known to the learner in advance. We say a concept class $\mathcal{H}$ is learnable if there is a learning algorithm $\mathcal{A}$, such that for every realizable sequence, the number of mistakes made by $\mathcal{A}$ grows at most sublinearly with the number of predictions.
